stories.headline: Scientists Just Grew Chickpeas in Moon Dirt — And They Survived
stories.hook: In a lab at Texas A&M, chickpea plants sprouted from simulated lunar soil and made actual seeds, which means hummus on the moon is now theoretically on the table.
stories.body:
Researchers have successfully grown chickpeas in simulated lunar regolith — the gray, rocky powder that covers the Moon's surface. The study, published in Scientific Reports in early March 2026, found that plants could survive in soil mixtures containing up to 75% simulated moon dirt, as long as the seeds were coated with helpful mycorrhizal fungi before planting.

The fungi are the secret ingredient. Arbuscular mycorrhizae form a symbiotic relationship with plant roots, shielding them from the toxic metals naturally present in lunar soil while helping absorb water and nutrients. Plants treated with fungi survived longer and even produced viable seeds — a first for any crop grown in moon-like conditions.

With NASA's Artemis program eyeing extended lunar stays, the question of how to feed astronauts long-term has become urgent. Shipping food from Earth costs roughly $1 million per kilogram. Chickpeas are an attractive candidate because they're high in protein, fix their own nitrogen from the air, and apparently have what it takes to grow somewhere nobody has ever lived.

image.alt: Green plant seedlings growing in soil
image.credit: Photo by Debby Ledet on Unsplash

links.title: Study: Chickpeas can grow in moon dirt and make seeds (Science News)

stories.headline: A Virus Found in a Tokyo Pond Might Rewrite the Origin of Complex Life
stories.hook: Scientists discovered a giant virus in a suburban Japanese pond that behaves suspiciously like the ancestor of every nucleus in every cell of every animal that ever lived.
stories.body:
A newly discovered giant virus called ushikuvirus — named after Ushiku-numa, the freshwater pond near Tokyo where it was found — is reigniting one of biology's most radical ideas: that the nucleus of complex cells may have originally been a virus.

The virus infects amoebae and does something unusual when it takes over: it forms a membrane-enclosed structure inside the host cell that behaves remarkably like a cell nucleus, complete with DNA replication happening inside a protective membrane. This mirrors the viral eukaryogenesis hypothesis, first proposed in 2001, which suggests that billions of years ago a large DNA virus infected an ancient single-celled organism and, instead of killing it, settled in permanently and became the nucleus.

If true, this would mean that every complex cell on Earth — in every mushroom, fish, oak tree, and human — has a virus to thank for its most important feature. The research team at Japan's National Institute of Natural Sciences published their findings in the Journal of Virology. While the hypothesis remains controversial, ushikuvirus offers the most compelling structural evidence yet that viruses didn't just infect life — they may have helped build it.

image.alt: Colorful bacterial colonies growing on a dark surface
image.credit: Photo by Guy Grandjean on Unsplash

links.title: Giant virus discovery could rewrite the origin of complex life (ScienceDaily)
New Giant Virus Found in Japan (SciTechDaily)

stories.headline: The Vegetables You Eat May Be Full of Other People's Antidepressants
stories.hook: Scientists studying crops irrigated with treated wastewater found that tomatoes, lettuce, and carrots are quietly absorbing antidepressants, seizure drugs, and other pharmaceuticals from the soil.
stories.body:
A growing portion of the world's crops are irrigated with treated wastewater — a practice that sounds sensible until you consider what's in that water after treatment. A new study has found that trace pharmaceuticals, including antidepressants, anticonvulsants, and antibiotics, survive water treatment and accumulate in the leaves and tissues of food plants.

The concentrations found are far below therapeutic doses, so eating a salad isn't going to sedate you. But researchers are concerned about long-term cumulative exposure, especially for people who eat large quantities of affected produce or who are particularly sensitive to pharmaceutical compounds. Children and pregnant women are flagged as populations that warrant closer study.

Tomatoes, carrots, and lettuce showed the highest uptake in preliminary tests, likely because of their high water content and rapid growth during irrigation periods. The findings have renewed calls for improved pharmaceutical filtration at wastewater treatment plants and updated regulatory standards for irrigation water. As water scarcity drives more regions toward treated-water irrigation, scientists say the issue will only grow more pressing.

image.alt: Red and green tomatoes growing in a garden
image.credit: Photo by Jonathan Kemper on Unsplash

links.title: New to Science discoveries March 2026 (Microbiology Society)

stories.headline: Archaeologists Have Found a Lost Ancient City on the Tigris River
stories.hook: A metropolis that vanished from history has been rediscovered in Iraq — and it may be the long-lost Alexandria on the Tigris, a city founded by Alexander the Great's successors.
stories.body:
In one of 2026's most significant archaeological finds, researchers have identified the ruins of a large ancient city buried along the Tigris River in modern-day Iraq. The site, believed to be the legendary Alexandria on the Tigris, was a major urban center during the Hellenistic and Parthian periods — roughly 300 BCE to 200 CE — before it was swallowed by time and sediment.

The city joins a remarkable year for archaeology. Egyptian archaeologist Zahi Hawass has also announced that a previously unknown chamber inside the Pyramid of Khufu at Giza will be formally revealed to the world in 2026, while excavations at Karahantepe in Turkey — a Neolithic site even older than Stonehenge — have uncovered a massive circular amphitheater-like structure from 9,000 BCE complete with carved stone benches and sculptures.

For the Tigris site, researchers used a combination of satellite imaging, ground-penetrating radar, and targeted excavation to map the city's street grid and major buildings. The find is particularly significant because Mesopotamia — the region between the Tigris and Euphrates rivers — is considered the cradle of civilization, and large portions of it remain unexcavated.

image.alt: Ancient ruins with palm trees under a blue sky
image.credit: Photo by Mr. Great Heart on Unsplash

stories.headline: Physical AI Is About to Escape the Lab — Here's What That Actually Means
stories.hook: At NVIDIA's biggest conference of the year, which kicks off tomorrow in San Jose, the talk isn't about chatbots anymore — it's about robots that learn in simulation and act in the real world.
stories.body:
NVIDIA GTC 2026, running March 16–19 in San Jose, has one dominant theme this year: physical AI — the branch of artificial intelligence that doesn't just generate text or images, but controls physical systems in the real world. Think robots, autonomous vehicles, and industrial machines that can perceive, reason, and act in messy, unpredictable environments.

The conference will feature sessions from leading robotics companies showing how they train robots entirely inside digital simulations before deploying them to physical hardware. The gap between simulated training and real-world performance — long the bane of robotics research — is reportedly closing fast, thanks to better physics simulators and foundation models that generalize across different physical environments.

In one of the more unexpected sessions, Disney will present how it is using physical AI to bring its beloved characters to life as actual robots — not just remote-controlled props, but machines with onboard AI that can interact naturally with park visitors. Meanwhile, NVIDIA's new Rubin GPU platform promises up to 10× lower cost per AI token compared to the previous generation, which could make intelligent robotics economically viable for a much wider range of industries than before.

image.alt: A modern black robot with blue and orange accents
image.credit: Photo by Luan Fonseca on Unsplash

links.title: A Robotics Community Guide to GTC San Jose 2026

stories.headline: The High Seas Just Got Their First Legal Protection in History
stories.hook: As of January 2026, the ocean beyond any country's jurisdiction — that's nearly half the planet's surface — finally has a governing law for the first time.
stories.body:
The High Seas Treaty, formally known as the BBNJ Agreement (Biodiversity Beyond National Jurisdiction), became legally binding on January 17, 2026, after nearly two decades of negotiations. It establishes for the first time a framework for governing marine life in international waters — the vast stretches of ocean that lie beyond any nation's 200-mile exclusive economic zone.

The treaty requires countries to conduct environmental impact assessments before undertaking activities in the high seas, establishes a system for creating marine protected areas in international waters, and includes provisions for sharing the benefits of any marine genetic resources that companies or researchers discover and commercialize.

Why does this matter? The high seas cover roughly 64% of the ocean's surface and are home to extraordinary biodiversity, including species that have never been seen by human eyes. They're also largely unprotected — historically treated as a global commons where fishing fleets, shipping companies, and deep-sea mining operations could operate with minimal oversight. Conservation scientists are calling the treaty a landmark moment, though they note that enforcement — always the hard part of international law — remains to be proven.

image.alt: Ocean waves crashing on shore during daytime
image.credit: Photo by Polina Silivanova on Unsplash

stories.headline: Mars Was Habitable Far Longer Than Scientists Thought — By Billions of Years
stories.hook: Ancient sand dunes in Mars's Gale Crater appear to have been soaked by underground water long after the planet was supposed to have dried out, which changes a lot of assumptions about life there.
stories.body:
New research suggests Mars may have remained habitable much longer than the scientific consensus held. Analysis of ancient sand dunes in Gale Crater — the area being explored by NASA's Curiosity rover — shows evidence that liquid water was present underground well into what scientists previously considered Mars's dry era.

The findings push back the potential window for life on Mars by hundreds of millions, possibly billions, of years. If water persisted underground even as the surface became arid, subsurface microbial life may have had an extraordinarily long time to develop and adapt. Curiosity has also recently been investigating strange spiderweb-like mineral ridges in the same region, which researchers believe may have formed through ancient chemical reactions between water and rock.

Mars scientists are also excited about a separate discovery: a possible new mineral hiding in the planet's ancient sulfate deposits. Sulfates typically form when water evaporates and are considered strong biosignature candidates — places where ancient life might have left chemical traces. The combination of extended liquid water history and unexplained minerals makes Mars feel more promising as an astrobiology target than it did just a few years ago.

image.alt: A person standing in the middle of a vast red desert
image.credit: Photo by Viktor Ozolin on Unsplash

stories.headline: Portugal Ran on 80% Renewable Energy in January — In the Middle of Winter
stories.hook: Not a single solar panel helped Portugal generate the vast majority of its electricity last January — it was all wind, hydro, and other renewables powering the country through the coldest months.
stories.body:
Portugal generated 80.7% of its electricity from renewable sources in January 2026, a remarkable achievement that's especially striking given that January is when solar output is at its lowest. The bulk of that energy came from wind turbines and hydroelectric dams, which together produced enough power to cover most of the country's needs with clean energy to spare.

Portugal has been quietly building toward this milestone for years, investing heavily in wind capacity along its Atlantic coast and modernizing its hydroelectric infrastructure. The country has also benefited from a wet winter, which kept reservoir levels high for hydro generation. In previous years, Portugal has hit 100% renewable electricity briefly during favorable weather windows, but sustaining such high percentages through an entire winter month marks a new level of grid reliability.

The achievement offers a real-world test case for what a largely renewable grid looks like in practice for a mid-sized European country. Grid managers note the remaining 19% gap is covered by natural gas and some imports, and that battery storage infrastructure will be the next key investment to reach consistent 90%+ renewable operation year-round.

image.alt: Solar panel and wind turbine on a pole against a blue sky
image.credit: Photo by Takahiro Nishizono on Unsplash

links.title: Environmental News in Brief: Spring 2026 (Earth Island)

stories.headline: A Rocky Planet Was Found Exactly Where Planetary Science Says It Shouldn't Be
stories.hook: ESA's CHEOPS telescope spotted a fourth planet in a nearby star system, and it's sitting in a spot that breaks nearly every existing model of how solar systems form.
stories.body:
The LHS 1903 star system just became significantly more interesting. ESA's CHEOPS space telescope has confirmed the existence of a fourth exoplanet in the system — and its location is baffling planetary scientists. Dubbed an 'inside-out' system, the rocky world sits right where planet formation models predict there shouldn't be enough material to build one.

Conventional planetary formation theory says rocky planets grow in the inner warm zones of a star system, while gas giants accumulate farther out where icy material is more abundant. The LHS 1903 configuration inverts this, with a configuration that challenges the standard story of how planetary systems evolve from swirling discs of gas and dust.

The CHEOPS mission, operated by the European Space Agency, is designed specifically for this kind of precision planet characterization — measuring tiny dips in starlight as planets pass in front of their host stars. The LHS 1903 finding is one of several recent 'weird' exoplanet configurations that astronomers are accumulating, each one adding pressure on theorists to revise how solar system formation is modeled. It's becoming clear that our own solar system may be the exception rather than the rule.

image.alt: A planet with glowing continents visible from dark space
image.credit: Photo by Bhautik Patel on Unsplash

stories.headline: Astronomers Just Caught the Brightest Radio Burst Ever — And Traced It to a Nearby Galaxy
stories.hook: A flash of radio energy billions of times more powerful than our Sun's entire output for that instant just lit up the sky, and for the first time scientists know exactly which galaxy sent it.
stories.body:
Fast radio bursts — FRBs — are among the most energetic and mysterious phenomena in the universe: millisecond-long flashes of radio waves that release as much energy in a fraction of a second as the Sun does in days. The latest one to hit Earth's detectors was the brightest ever recorded, and thanks to a newly operational network of CHIME Outrigger telescopes, astronomers were able to triangulate its source to a specific galaxy just a cosmological stone's throw away.

The ability to pinpoint FRB sources is a major leap forward. Previously, most bursts could only be localized to broad patches of sky, making it difficult to study what kinds of environments and events produce them. The CHIME Outrigger network — a set of satellite dishes spread across North America that work together to achieve higher resolution — is changing that.

The source galaxy's proximity also means scientists can study it in detail with other telescopes to understand what's happening there. Leading theories for FRB origins include magnetars (incredibly dense, magnetized neutron stars), merging compact objects, and exotic astrophysical events that haven't yet been fully characterized. Each new well-localized burst gives theorists more data to work with.

image.alt: The Karl G. Jansky Very Large Array radio telescope dishes in a field

stories.headline: Apple's New AI-Powered Siri Is Actually Going to Be Google's AI
stories.hook: In a twist nobody saw coming, Apple is reportedly partnering with its longtime rival Google to power the next version of Siri with Google's Gemini model — run privately on Apple hardware.
stories.body:
Apple is planning to use Google's Gemini AI model to power a dramatically redesigned Siri, according to multiple reports ahead of an expected iOS 26.4 release this spring. The partnership is surprising given Apple and Google's history as fierce competitors, but it makes a certain strategic sense: Apple gets access to a state-of-the-art large language model without having to build one itself, while Google gains a major distribution deal.

The privacy angle is central to Apple's pitch. Rather than sending user requests to Google's servers, the system is designed to run on Apple's Private Cloud Compute infrastructure — dedicated hardware that processes requests without allowing Apple or Google employees to see the data. Whether that privacy promise will satisfy regulators and skeptical users remains an open question.

Apple has reportedly held similar conversations with OpenAI and Anthropic, suggesting the company is keeping its options open rather than committing to a single AI partner long-term. For users, the practical implication is a Siri that can finally handle complex, multi-step requests — the kind of thing that has made rival AI assistants increasingly attractive over the past two years.

image.alt: Hands holding a smartphone displaying a list of articles
image.credit: Photo by Matúš Gocman on Unsplash

links.title: What's next for AI in 2026 (MIT Technology Review)

stories.headline: City Trees Are Making Some Cities Hotter, Not Cooler
stories.hook: A massive study of 761 megacities found that urban trees and green spaces — the supposed antidote to the urban heat island effect — actually cause net warming in roughly one in four cities.
stories.body:
Trees are widely prescribed as a solution to urban heat islands — the phenomenon where cities become significantly hotter than surrounding countryside due to all that pavement and concrete absorbing sunlight. So a new global study covering 761 megacities across 105 countries landed like a surprise: in arid and semi-arid cities that receive less than 1,000 millimeters of rainfall per year, vegetation can cause net warming rather than cooling.

The mechanism is counterintuitive but makes physical sense. In dry climates, plants transpire water from their leaves — a cooling process. But when water is scarce, that evaporative cooling is limited. Meanwhile, trees can trap heat by reducing wind flow and absorbing solar radiation in their canopies, with less of it radiating back to space compared to bare ground. In very dry cities, this trapping effect can outweigh the transpiration cooling, leaving the city warmer on balance.

The findings don't mean cities should rip out their trees. Trees provide crucial services beyond temperature regulation — air filtration, mental health benefits, habitat, stormwater management. But the study suggests that cities in arid regions need more targeted strategies: drought-adapted tree species, careful placement to maximize shade on pedestrian areas, and complementary cooling infrastructure.

image.alt: Calm lake surrounded by trees with distant city buildings
image.credit: Photo by Duy Le Duc on Unsplash

links.title: Climate change is accelerating but nature is slowing down (ScienceDaily)
Conservation's hot topics of 2026 (The Invading Sea)

stories.headline: Disney Is Building Real AI-Powered Robotic Characters — Not Puppets, Actual Robots
stories.hook: At NVIDIA's GTC conference this week, Disney will reveal how its imagineers are using artificial intelligence and physical simulation to create robot versions of beloved characters that can genuinely interact with people.
stories.body:
Disney has long used animatronics — mechanically controlled figures — to bring characters to life in its theme parks. But the new generation of Disney robots being developed under the physical AI banner is fundamentally different. These systems use onboard AI to perceive their environment, understand social context, and generate natural-seeming responses in real time, rather than playing back scripted animations.

At NVIDIA GTC 2026 this week, Disney's robotics team will present 'Disney's Robotic Characters: From the Screen to Reality via Physical AI,' revealing details of how simulation environments are used to train robot behaviors before the robots ever enter a physical park. Training in simulation allows thousands of hours of experience to accumulate without the wear, cost, or safety risks of live robot testing.

The goal, according to Disney, is characters that feel genuinely alive — ones that can make eye contact, respond to a child's movements and words, and maintain character consistency across an interaction. This is a significant departure from the scripted, looping behavior of traditional animatronics. The broader implications for entertainment, retail, and hospitality industries are significant: if Disney can make this work reliably at scale, it suggests interactive robotic companions could become much more common in public spaces within the next decade.

image.alt: People gathered at a Hollywood-style entertainment venue entrance at night
image.credit: Photo by Gurdaas Malik on Unsplash

links.title: Join the Physical AI Developer Meetup at GTC 2026

stories.headline: Tech Has Already Cut 45,000 Jobs in 2026 — Despite the AI Boom
stories.hook: The technology industry is paradoxically laying off tens of thousands of workers in the same year that AI capabilities are advancing faster than ever, and the two facts are not unrelated.
stories.body:
The tech industry has shed approximately 45,000 jobs in the first three months of 2026, making this one of the most concentrated periods of layoffs since the post-pandemic correction of 2022–2023. The cuts span companies large and small, from established cloud computing giants trimming middle management to AI startups running out of runway.

The picture is more nuanced than a simple 'AI is replacing workers' narrative. Many of the layoffs are in roles that companies ramped up aggressively during the remote-work era and are now normalizing. Others reflect a broader restructuring as companies shift investment toward AI infrastructure and away from traditional software development headcount. Hiring hasn't stopped — it's become highly concentrated in AI engineering, infrastructure, and safety roles.

For individual workers caught in the cuts, the restructuring is disorienting. Skills that commanded premium salaries two years ago — certain types of software development, content moderation, some data analysis roles — are becoming commoditized faster than people can retrain. Labor economists note that historically, technology-driven job disruption has eventually created more jobs than it destroyed, but 'eventually' offers little comfort in the short term. The pattern playing out in 2026 is likely to accelerate as AI agent capabilities continue to expand.

image.alt: Man in yellow sweater working on a laptop at a desk
image.credit: Photo by Vitaly Gariev on Unsplash

links.title: 2026: The year AI moves from experimentation to execution (Innovation News Network)
Morgan Stanley warns an AI breakthrough is coming (Fortune)

stories.headline: Ancient Sea Sponges Were Earth's First Animals — MIT Has the Chemical Proof
stories.hook: Scientists at MIT have found molecular evidence locked inside 541-million-year-old rocks that Earth's first animals weren't worms or jellyfish — they were simple, motionless sea sponges.
stories.body:
The question of which animal came first has long been argued using fossils, but fossils of soft-bodied organisms rarely survive. MIT researchers have taken a different approach, hunting for chemical biomarkers — molecular fossils that can persist in rocks for hundreds of millions of years even after all physical traces of the organisms have vanished.

In rocks dating to more than 541 million years ago, from before the Cambrian explosion that produced most of the major animal body plans we recognize today, the team found a specific sterol compound called 26-methylstigmastane. This molecule is produced by modern demosponges — the most common type of sea sponge today — and appears to be unique to that lineage. Its presence in ancient rocks suggests sponges were present and thriving before all other animals.

The finding matters beyond settling a biological dispute. Sponges are filter feeders that pump enormous volumes of water through their bodies, oxygenating the ocean around them. If sponges came first, they may have helped create the environmental conditions — more oxygen, better nutrient cycling — that made it possible for more complex animals to evolve in the first place. The sponges didn't just show up first; they may have built the stage on which the rest of animal life could appear.

image.alt: An underwater view of corals and sponges on the ocean floor
image.credit: Photo by Karl Callwood on Unsplash
